description: The currently displayed image is an irrelevant user interface button marked with the text 'Check for updates', which is not the actual research figure from the Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel decomposition study. No valid material science related information, including microstructure characterization results, mechanical property data, phase analysis outcomes, or processing route diagrams corresponding to the paper's research on austenite decomposition behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels under 600℃ and 800℃ tempering conditions, can be extracted from this incorrect image. The image does not match the described content of the target paper at all.

This set of optical micrographs (both with 100 μm scale bar) displays the discontinuous precipitation microstructures of two Fe-9Al-10Mn lightweight steels tempered at 600 °C. Subfigure (a) corresponds to the 0.4 wt% C containing alloy (9Al-10Mn-0.4C), where austenite grains only undergo partial discontinuous precipitation, with large volumes of untransformed austenite grains remaining. Subfigure (b) corresponds to the 1.0 wt% C containing alloy (9Al-10Mn-1C), where almost the entire volume of austenite is transformed into γ + κ-carbide lamellar structures, with only a few band-shaped residual austenite regions observed. The comparison demonstrates that higher carbon content significantly promotes the extent of discontinuous precipitation during 600 °C tempering, which enhances the hardness of the steels. This figure supports the conclusion that chemical composition, particularly carbon content, is a critical factor controlling tempering phase transformation behavior and mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels.

These two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images illustrate the discontinuous precipitation behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels after tempering at 600 °C. The first image for 9Al-10Mn-0.4C alloy (magnification ×10000, scale bar 1μm) shows that discontinuous precipitation of γ + κ-carbide lamellae initiates at grain boundaries, with limited volume of transformed austenite. The second image for 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy (magnification ×10000, scale bar 1μm) presents that most austenite is transformed via discontinuous precipitation, forming finer κ-carbide lamellae with smaller inter-lamellar spacing. Comparison confirms that higher carbon content significantly promotes the occurrence and propagation of discontinuous precipitation reaction, and the finer lamellar structure in higher C alloy leads to evident hardening effect, verifying the critical role of C content in tuning phase transformation and mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels under 600 °C tempering.

These two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images, both captured at ×10,000 magnification with a 1 μm scale bar, present the microstructures of two Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels after 600 °C tempering heat treatment. Subfigure (a) corresponds to 2Al-10Mn-0.4C steel, showing a multifaceted microstructure consisting of irregular lath-like and dispersed globular precipitates within the matrix. Subfigure (b) corresponds to 12Al-10Mn-1C steel, exhibiting regularly aligned elongated precipitates, with no lamellar γ + κ-carbide structures detected, only globular κ-carbides embedded in the austenite matrix alongside carbide precipitation along δ/γ boundaries. The comparison confirms that higher Al content increases the driving force for austenite decomposition, enabling full-volume phase transformation during 600 °C tempering, and the absence of random lamellar phases delivers a more stable microstructure configuration.

This is an optical micrograph showing the precipitation transformation microstructure of 12Al-10Mn-1C Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel after tempering at 600 °C for 30 min, with a scale bar of 100 μm. Two distinct microstructure regions are labelled: the γ+κ lamellae region, and the alternating banded region composed of γᵈ austenite and coarse κ carbide. The observed transformation is a complex random-like precipitation transformation rather than ordered discontinuous precipitation, which is associated with slight hardening effect of the alloy. The high 12 wt% Al content increases the thermodynamic gradient between hot rolling and tempering equilibrium, reduces austenite stability, and promotes austenite decomposition during tempering. This figure confirms that generic precipitation transformation occurs in 12Al-10Mn-1C under 600 °C tempering, producing mixed lamellar and banded multiphase microstructures.

This figure displays microstructures resulting from cellular transformation and spinodal decomposition in four Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels after tempering at 800 °C. The upper row of each subfigure is optical microscopy (OM) micrograph for low-magnification overview, and the lower row is scanning electron microscopy (SEM) secondary electron image for high-resolution microstructural characterization. Subfigures (a) to (d) correspond to 9Al-10Mn-1C, 12Al-10Mn-1C, 12Al-20Mn-1C, and 12Al-30Mn-1C alloys respectively. 9Al-10Mn-1C shows mixed κ+α+γ lamellar structures from cellular transformation and partial spinodal decomposed regions; 12Al-10Mn-1C presents fully cellular transformation-derived κ-carbide lamellae; 12Al-20Mn-1C features early-stage cellular transformation at grain boundaries plus partial spinodal decomposition; 12Al-30Mn-1C exhibits fully spinodal decomposed austenite with nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates. The figure verifies that increasing Mn content shifts the dominant austenite transformation from cellular transformation to spinodal decomposition, and both transformations contribute to hardness enhancement of the steels.

This set of 4 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images characterize the microstructure of Fe-9Al-10Mn-1C lightweight steel after annealing at 800 °C for 30 min followed by water quenching. The first image (1000× magnification, 10 μm scale bar) and second image (5000× magnification, 5 μm scale bar) show regions undergoing spinodal decomposition, with homogeneously distributed nano-sized κ-carbides precipitated in the matrix. The third image (5000× magnification, 5 μm scale bar) and fourth image (10000× magnification, 1 μm scale bar) present the lamellar structure formed via cellular transformation, composed of κ-carbides, α-ferrite and residual austenite. These observations confirm that two distinct phase transformation mechanisms coexist in this alloy at 800 °C: cellular transformation dominates for most austenite fraction, while spinodal decomposition occurs in the remaining austenite, which contributes to the outstanding hardness of this alloy among 9 wt% Al-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels.

This is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image captured at 5000× magnification with a 5 μm scale bar, showing the microstructure of 12Al-20Mn-1C Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel after tempering at 800 °C. The image displays wide, thick lath-like κ-carbide lamellae, which are products of full austenite decomposition via combined cellular transformation and spinodal decomposition. The κ-carbide strips in this 12 wt% Al alloy are confirmed to be wider and thicker than those observed in 9 wt% Al alloy variants from related study figures. Even with relatively large inter-lamellar spacing, this lamellar carbide structure delivers consistent hardening effects for the alloy, supporting the conclusion that Al content modulates κ-carbide morphology during high-temperature tempering of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels.

This set of 6 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images illustrates the morphology of nano-sized κ-carbides generated via spinodal decomposition in Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels after tempering at 800 °C. Subfigures (a, b), with magnifications of ×10,000 (1 μm scale bar) and ×30,000 (0.5 μm scale bar) respectively, show agglomerated thin acicular κ-carbides distributed in a medium-density grid in the 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy. Subfigures (c, d) (same magnification series as a, b) present the densest cuboidal κ-carbide configuration in the 12Al-20Mn-1C alloy. Subfigures (e, f) display finer, less dense acicular-like agglomerated κ-carbides in the 12Al-30Mn-1C alloy. This figure confirms that Al and Mn contents significantly modulate the morphology, size, and distribution of spinodal-derived κ-carbides, supporting the conclusion that chemical composition regulates spinodal decomposition behavior and precipitate characteristics in Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels.

This figure presents phase amount (in mol) as a function of temperature (0 to 1300 °C) for two Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels, to demonstrate thermodynamic gradients of austenite stability. Subplot (a) corresponds to 9Al-10Mn-0.4C alloy, while subplot (b) corresponds to 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy. Three phases are tracked: BCC_A2 ferrite, FCC_A1 austenite, and KAPPA_E21 κ-carbide. For (a), BCC_A2 is the dominant phase at low temperatures with ~0.08 mol κ-carbide; above ~700 °C, BCC_A2 content drops sharply, FCC_A1 content rises to ~0.48 mol at 850 °C, and κ-carbide disappears rapidly. For (b), BCC_A2 dominates low temperatures with ~0.21 mol κ-carbide; above ~700 °C, BCC_A2 content drops, FCC_A1 content rises to ~1.0 mol at 950-1050 °C, and κ-carbide diminishes gradually. The figure confirms that carbon content alters phase equilibrium, and austenite decomposition is controlled by both chemical composition and tempering temperature, supporting that both alloy design and heat treatment regulate phase transformation behavior in lightweight steels.

This figure shows two scanning electron microscopy micrographs of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels subjected to 600 °C tempering treatment, each with a 20 μm scale bar. Subfigure (a) corresponds to 9Al-20Mn-1C alloy, while subfigure (b) corresponds to 9Al-30Mn-1C alloy. Both samples exhibit equiaxed austenite grains, with parallel grain striations of distinct orientations observed inside individual grains for both alloys. The 9Al-20Mn-1C sample in (a) displays continuous dark precipitate features along grain boundaries, while the 9Al-30Mn-1C sample in (b) has finer, discrete grain boundary carbides. As stated in the caption, the 9Al-20Mn-1C sample shows precipitates continuous with austenite grains, while 9Al-30Mn-1C only contains isolated carbides. This figure supports the conclusion that these striated microstructures do not produce the expected hardening effect from carbide precipitation, unlike the fine lamellar precipitate microstructure in lower Mn 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy, indicating potential detrimental effects on strength and ductility.

This is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image captured at 20 kV acceleration voltage, 5000× magnification, with a 5 μm scale bar. The tested sample is Fe-9Al-20Mn-1C lightweight steel after 600 °C tempering treatment. The image shows carbides distributed along grain boundaries, with clear evidence of structural continuity between the carbide phase and δ ferrite grains. The carbide phase presents a continuous band-like morphology at grain boundary triple junctions and along grain boundary paths, while flat matrix grain boundaries and tiny microcracks are observed in the background. This figure confirms that grain boundary carbide precipitation occurs during 600 °C tempering of high-Al high-Mn lightweight steels, supporting the investigation of austenite decomposition behaviors in this alloy system under medium-temperature heat treatment conditions.

This figure presents four phase volume fraction diagrams for Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels with four different nominal compositions: (a) 9Al-20Mn-1C, (b) 9Al-30Mn-1C, (c) 12Al-20Mn-1C, (d) 12Al-30Mn-1C. The x-axis represents temperature from 0 ℃ to 1300 ℃, the y-axis indicates the molar amount of each phase, including FCC_A1 (austenite), BCC_A2 (ferrite), KAPPA_E21 (κ-carbide), BCC_2SL, and LIQUID (appeared in partial subplots). Key trends are identified: for 9 wt% Al alloys, the austenite fraction at 800 ℃ is higher than that at 1150 ℃, while the trend reverses for 12 wt% Al alloys, as higher Al content shifts the austenite stability region to higher temperatures and reduces its stability at common tempering temperatures. This figure supports the core conclusion that higher Al addition increases austenite decomposition driving force during tempering, leading to easier phase transformation in 12Al series steels.

This set of three optical micrographs, with a consistent 400 μm scale bar for each sub-figure, presents the microstructures of three Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel grades after tempering at 800 °C: sub-figure (a) is for 9Al-10Mn-1C, (b) for 12Al-20Mn-1C, and (c) for 12Al-30Mn-1C. Clear microstructural differences are visible: for lower Mn grades 9Al-10Mn-1C (a) and 12Al-20Mn-1C (b), cellular transformation products are dominant over spinodal decomposition products due to lower austenite stability, with alternately layered morphology in (a) and mixed discrete dark phase bands and spinodal regions in (b). For the highest Mn grade 12Al-30Mn-1C (c), the entire austenitic volume undergoes spinodal decomposition, forming a uniform fine modulated structure. This figure validates that higher Mn content elevates austenite stability, favoring spinodal decomposition instead of cellular transformation during 800 °C tempering of the studied steels.

This figure contains two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images characterizing the microstructure of 12Al-20Mn-1C Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel after tempering at 800 °C for 30 min. The first image, acquired at ×2500 magnification with a 10 μm scale bar, displays the overall morphology of ferrite branches growing within spinodal decomposed austenite (γ) grains. The second image, captured at ×10000 magnification with a 1 μm scale bar, shows the fine structure of these ferrite branches and the surrounding spinodal decomposed matrix with distributed nano-sized κ-carbide precipitates. These observations confirm that partial grains of the 800 °C-tempered 12Al-20Mn-1C alloy have mixed microstructures, where ferrite branches coexist with spinodal decomposition products, supporting the conclusion that Mn content regulates austenite decomposition pathways, with both cellular transformation and spinodal decomposition occurring in this alloy at 800 °C.
